Motivation: Existing ergodic trajectory optimization methods struggle with point-cloud targets due to nonconvex optimization landscapes and inadequate handling of SE(3) constraints in sampling-as-optimization techniques for surface manipulation tasks.

Method: Proposes a preconditioned SE(3) Stein Variational Gradient Descent approach with three innovations: 1) reformulating point-cloud ergodic coverage as manifold-aware sampling, 2) deriving SE(3)-specific SVGD particle updates, and 3) developing a preconditioner to accelerate trajectory optimization convergence.

Result: The method consistently identifies superior local optima compared to optimization-based and SAO baselines while preserving SE(3) geometric structure. Experiments on 3D point-cloud surface coverage benchmark and robotic surface drawing tasks show superior coverage quality with tractable computation.

Conclusion: The proposed preconditioned SE(3) SVGD approach effectively addresses SE(3) constraints in ergodic trajectory generation for surface manipulation, outperforming existing methods and validating in real-world robot experiments.

Abstract: Surface manipulation tasks require robots to generate trajectories that comprehensively cover complex 3D surfaces while maintaining precise end-effector poses. Existing ergodic trajectory optimization (TO) methods demonstrate success in coverage tasks, while struggling with point-cloud targets due to the nonconvex optimization landscapes and the inadequate handling of SE(3) constraints in sampling-as-optimization (SAO) techniques. In this work, we introduce a preconditioned SE(3) Stein Variational Gradient Descent (SVGD) approach for SAO ergodic trajectory generation. Our proposed approach comprises multiple innovations. First, we reformulate point-cloud ergodic coverage as a manifold-aware sampling problem. Second, we derive SE(3)-specific SVGD particle updates, and, third, we develop a preconditioner to accelerate TO convergence. Our sampling-based framework consistently identifies superior local optima compared to strong optimization-based and SAO baselines while preserving the SE(3) geometric structure. Experiments on a 3D point-cloud surface coverage benchmark and robotic surface drawing tasks demonstrate that our method achieves superior coverage quality with tractable computation in our setting relative to existing TO and SAO approaches, and is validated in real-world robot experiments.

</details>


### [245] [SEA-Nav: Efficient Policy Learning for Safe and Agile Quadruped Navigation in Cluttered Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.09460)
*Shiyi Chen,Mingye Yang,Haiyan Mao,Jiaqi Zhang,Haiyi Liu,Shuheng He,Debing Zhang,Zihao Qiu,Chun Zhang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: SEA-Nav: A reinforcement learning framework for quadruped robots that achieves safe, efficient, and agile navigation in dense clutter with minute-level training time.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing quadruped navigation methods have limitations: they either lack safety and agility in simple obstacle distributions, suffer from slow locomotion in complex environments, or require excessively long training phases. There's a need for a solution that can handle dense clutter while being both safe and efficient to train.

Method: Uses reinforcement learning with three key components: 1) A differentiable control barrier function (CBF)-based shield to constrain navigation policy to output safe velocity commands, 2) An adaptive collision replay mechanism and hazardous exploration rewards to increase learning from critical experiences, and 3) Kinematic action constraints to ensure safe velocity commands for physical deployment.

Result: Achieves highly challenging quadruped navigation in the real world with minute-level training time, which is the first approach to accomplish this according to the authors.

Conclusion: SEA-Nav successfully addresses the challenge of efficiently training quadruped navigation in densely cluttered environments by combining safety guarantees through CBF shielding, efficient learning through adaptive replay and reward design, and practical deployability through kinematic constraints.

Abstract: Efficiently training quadruped robot navigation in densely cluttered environments remains a significant challenge. Existing methods are either limited by a lack of safety and agility in simple obstacle distributions or suffer from slow locomotion in complex environments, often requiring excessively long training phases. To this end, we propose SEA-Nav (Safe, Efficient, and Agile Navigation), a reinforcement learning framework for quadruped navigation. Within diverse and dense obstacle environments, a differentiable control barrier function (CBF)-based shield constraints the navigation policy to output safe velocity commands. An adaptive collision replay mechanism and hazardous exploration rewards are introduced to increase the probability of learning from critical experiences, guiding efficient exploration and exploitation. Finally, kinematic action constraints are incorporated to ensure safe velocity commands, facilitating successful physical deployment. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first approach that achieves highly challenging quadruped navigation in the real world with minute-level training time.

</details>


### [246] [Receptogenesis in a Vascularized Robotic Embodiment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.09473)
*Kadri-Ann Pankratov,Leonid Zinatullin,Hans Priks,Adele Metsniit,Urmas Johanson,Tarmo Tamm,Alvo Aabloo,Edoardo Sinibaldi,Indrek Must*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Robots can grow their own sensors on-demand using internal fluidic systems and UV polymerization, enabling real-time hardware adaptation for emerging behaviors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current robotic systems lack the ability to physically restructure their hardware during operation. The paper aims to enable robots to generate new hardware components on-demand, inspired by biological circulatory systems that redistribute mass and function.

Method: Developed a vascularized robotic composite with fluidic channels containing photopolymerizable precursors. Used coordinated fluidic transport of precursors with localized UV irradiation to drive in situ photopolymerization, converting precursors into UV-sensitive polypyrrole sensors within the vasculature.

Result: Successfully demonstrated receptogenesis - on-demand construction of sensors from internal fluid reserves. The synthesized sensors showed characteristic decrease in electrical impedance and were used to close a control loop regulating wing flapping in a moth-inspired robot, increasing the robot's capability in real time.

Conclusion: Established a materials-based framework for constitutive evolution in robotics, enabling robots to physically grow hardware needed for emerging behaviors. This suggests pathways toward autonomous systems capable of generating specialized features like neurovascular systems in situated robotics.

Abstract: Equipping robotic systems with the capacity to generate $\textit{ex novo}$ hardware during operation extends control of physical adaptability. Unlike modular systems that rely on discrete component integration pre- or post-deployment, we envision the possibility that physical adaptation and development emerge from dynamic material restructuring to shape the body's intrinsic functions. Drawing inspiration from circulatory systems that redistribute mass and function in biological organisms, we utilize fluidics to restructure the material interface, a capability currently unpaired in robotics. Here, we realize this synthetic growth capability through a vascularized robotic composite designed for programmable material synthesis, demonstrated via receptogenesis - the on-demand construction of sensors from internal fluid reserves based on environmental cues. By coordinating the fluidic transport of precursors with external localized UV irradiation, we drive an $\textit{in situ}$ photopolymerization that chemically reconstructs the vasculature from the inside out. This reaction converts precursors with photolatent initiator into a solid dispersion of UV-sensitive polypyrrole, establishing a sensing modality validated by a characteristic decrease in electrical impedance. The newly synthesized sensor closed a control loop to regulate wing flapping in a moth-inspired robotic demonstrator. This physical update increased the robot's capability in real time. This work establishes a materials-based framework for constitutive evolution, enabling robots to physically grow the hardware needed to support emerging behaviors in a complex environment; for example, suggesting a pathway toward autonomous systems capable of generating specialized features, such as neurovascular systems in situated robotics.

</details>


### [247] [StyleVLA: Driving Style-Aware Vision Language Action Model for Autonomous Driving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.09482)
*Yuan Gao,Dengyuan Hua,Mattia Piccinini,Finn Rasmus Schäfer,Korbinian Moller,Lin Li,Johannes Betz*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: StyleVLA is a physics-informed Vision Language Action model that generates diverse, physically plausible driving trajectories with different driving styles, outperforming proprietary models like Gemini-3-Pro.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing VLA models for autonomous driving mainly generate generic collision-free trajectories but lack adaptation to diverse driving styles and often produce kinematically infeasible actions due to naive token prediction approaches.

Method: StyleVLA introduces a physics-informed VLA framework with hybrid loss combining kinematic consistency constraint and continuous regression head. Built on Qwen3-VL-4B, trained on large-scale dataset with 1.2k scenarios, 76k BEV samples, 42k FPV samples covering five driving styles with natural-language instructions.

Result: StyleVLA significantly outperforms proprietary models (Gemini-3-Pro) and state-of-the-art VLA models. Achieves composite driving scores of 0.55 on BEV and 0.51 on FPV, versus 0.32 and 0.35 for Gemini-3-Pro, measuring success rate, physical feasibility, and style adherence.

Conclusion: A specialized, physics-informed, lightweight model can surpass closed-source models on domain-specific autonomous driving tasks, demonstrating the value of incorporating physical constraints and driving style diversity in VLA frameworks.

Abstract: Vision Language Models (VLMs) bridge visual perception and linguistic reasoning. In Autonomous Driving (AD), this synergy has enabled Vision Language Action (VLA) models, which translate high-level multimodal understanding into driving behaviors, typically represented as future trajectories. However, existing VLA models mainly generate generic collision-free trajectories. Beyond collision avoidance, adapting to diverse driving styles (e.g., sporty, comfortable) is essential for personalized driving. Moreover, many methods treat trajectory generation as naive token prediction, which can produce kinematically infeasible actions. To address these limitations, we present StyleVLA, a physics-informed VLA framework for generating diverse and physically plausible driving behaviors. We introduce a hybrid loss that combines a kinematic consistency constraint with a continuous regression head to improve trajectory feasibility. To train StyleVLA, built on Qwen3-VL-4B, we construct a large-scale instruction dataset with over 1.2k scenarios, 76k Bird's Eye View (BEV) samples, and 42k First Person View (FPV) samples, with ground-truth trajectories for five driving styles and natural-language instructions. Experiments show that our 4B-parameter StyleVLA significantly outperforms proprietary models (e.g., Gemini-3-Pro) and state-of-the-art VLA models. Using a composite driving score measuring success rate, physical feasibility, and style adherence, StyleVLA achieves 0.55 on BEV and 0.51 on FPV, versus 0.32 and 0.35 for Gemini-3-Pro. These results show that a specialized, physics-informed, lightweight model can surpass closed-source models on domain-specific tasks.

</details>


### [248] [Beyond Short-Horizon: VQ-Memory for Robust Long-Horizon Manipulation in Non-Markovian Simulation Benchmarks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.09513)
*Wang Honghui,Jing Zhi,Ao Jicong,Song Shiji,Li Xuelong,Huang Gao,Bai Chenjia*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: RuleSafe is a new articulated manipulation benchmark with diverse safe unlocking tasks requiring multi-stage reasoning, plus VQ-Memory - a compact temporal representation using VQ-VAEs to encode past proprioceptive states for improved long-horizon planning in VLAs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing robotic simulation benchmarks focus on simple pick-and-place tasks, lacking the non-Markovian characteristics and articulated object complexity of real-world tasks. Real-robot data collection is expensive, creating need for better simulation benchmarks.

Method: 1) RuleSafe benchmark with LLM-generated safes featuring diverse unlocking mechanisms (key locks, password locks, logic locks) requiring multi-stage reasoning. 2) VQ-Memory representation using vector-quantized VAEs to encode past proprioceptive states into discrete latent tokens, filtering noise while preserving task-phase context.

Result: VQ-Memory consistently improves long-horizon planning, enhances generalization to unseen configurations, and enables more efficient manipulation with reduced computational cost in state-of-the-art VLA models and diffusion policies.

Conclusion: RuleSafe addresses limitations of existing benchmarks by introducing complex articulated manipulation tasks, while VQ-Memory provides an effective temporal representation that enhances robotic manipulation capabilities in non-Markovian, long-horizon scenarios.

Abstract: The high cost of collecting real-robot data has made robotic simulation a scalable platform for both evaluation and data generation. Yet most existing benchmarks concentrate on simple manipulation tasks such as pick-and-place, failing to capture the non-Markovian characteristics of real-world tasks and the complexity of articulated object interactions. To address this limitation, we present RuleSafe, a new articulated manipulation benchmark built upon a scalable LLM-aided simulation framework. RuleSafe features safes with diverse unlocking mechanisms, such as key locks, password locks, and logic locks, which require different multi-stage reasoning and manipulation strategies. These LLM-generated rules produce non-Markovian and long-horizon tasks that require temporal modeling and memory-based reasoning. We further propose VQ-Memory, a compact and structured temporal representation that uses vector-quantized variational autoencoders (VQ-VAEs) to encode past proprioceptive states into discrete latent tokens. This representation filters low-level noise while preserving high-level task-phase context, providing lightweight yet robust temporal cues that are compatible with existing Vision-Language-Action models (VLA). Extensive experiments on state-of-the-art VLA models and diffusion policies show that VQ-Memory consistently improves long-horizon planning, enhances generalization to unseen configurations, and enables more efficient manipulation with reduced computational cost. Project page: vqmemory.github.io

</details>


### [249] [NS-VLA: Towards Neuro-Symbolic Vision-Language-Action Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.09542)
*Ziyue Zhu,Shangyang Wu,Shuai Zhao,Zhiqiu Zhao,Shengjie Li,Yi Wang,Fang Li,Haoran Luo*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: NS-VLA is a neuro-symbolic framework that combines vision-language features with symbolic reasoning and online RL to improve robotic manipulation with better generalization, data efficiency, and exploration.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current VLA models struggle with learning reusable primitives, require large datasets and complex architectures, and have limited exploration beyond demonstrations.

Method: Proposes NS-VLA with: 1) symbolic encoder for vision-language feature embedding and primitive extraction, 2) symbolic solver for data-efficient action sequencing, and 3) online RL for optimization and exploration.

Result: Outperforms previous methods on robotic manipulation benchmarks in one-shot training and data-perturbed settings, with superior zero-shot generalization, high data efficiency, and expanded exploration space.

Conclusion: NS-VLA effectively addresses VLA challenges through neuro-symbolic integration with online RL, enabling more efficient and generalizable robotic manipulation.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models are formulated to ground instructions in visual context and generate action sequences for robotic manipulation. Despite recent progress, VLA models still face challenges in learning related and reusable primitives, reducing reliance on large-scale data and complex architectures, and enabling exploration beyond demonstrations. To address these challenges, we propose a novel Neuro-Symbolic Vision-Language-Action (NS-VLA) framework via online reinforcement learning (RL). It introduces a symbolic encoder to embedding vision and language features and extract structured primitives, utilizes a symbolic solver for data-efficient action sequencing, and leverages online RL to optimize generation via expansive exploration. Experiments on robotic manipulation benchmarks demonstrate that NS-VLA outperforms previous methods in both one-shot training and data-perturbed settings, while simultaneously exhibiting superior zero-shot generalizability, high data efficiency and expanded exploration space. Our code is available.

</details>


### [250] [On the Cost of Evolving Task Specialization in Multi-Robot Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.09552)
*Paolo Leopardi,Heiko Hamann,Jonas Kuckling,Tanja Katharina Kaiser*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Task specialization doesn't always improve swarm efficiency when optimization budget is limited - evolved generalists outperform specialists in foraging scenario.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Previous work showed task specialization emerges in evolution but focused on feasibility, not costs. Need cost-benefit analysis of specialization in robot swarms.

Method: Evolved artificial neural networks as both generalist behaviors (entire foraging task) and task-specialist behaviors (subtasks) within limited evaluation budget.

Result: Generalist behaviors successfully optimized, but task-specialist controllers failed to cooperate efficiently, resulting in worse performance than generalists.

Conclusion: Task specialization doesn't necessarily improve efficiency when optimization budget is limited; generalist approaches may be more effective under resource constraints.

Abstract: Task specialization can lead to simpler robot behaviors and higher efficiency in multi-robot systems. Previous works have shown the emergence of task specialization during evolutionary optimization, focusing on feasibility rather than costs. In this study, we take first steps toward a cost-benefit analysis of task specialization in robot swarms using a foraging scenario. We evolve artificial neural networks as generalist behaviors for the entire task and as task-specialist behaviors for subtasks within a limited evaluation budget. We show that generalist behaviors can be successfully optimized while the evolved task-specialist controllers fail to cooperate efficiently, resulting in worse performance than the generalists. Consequently, task specialization does not necessarily improve efficiency when optimization budget is limited.

</details>


### [251] [Trajectory Optimization for Self-Wrap-Aware Cable-Towed Planar Object Manipulation under Implicit Tension Constraints](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.09557)
*Yu Li,Amin Fakhari,Hamid Sadeghian*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper presents a tensioning-implicit trajectory optimization (TITO) framework for cable-towed manipulation that accounts for self-wrap contact dynamics, where the tether can wrap around object edges and change wrench transmission channels.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Cable/rope elements in deformable-object manipulation have complex unilateral and hybrid transmission characteristics where tethers can only pull when taut and become force-free when slack. The tether may also contact object boundaries and self-wrap around edges, which fundamentally changes wrench transmission by shifting effective application points and moment arms, coupling routing geometry with rigid-body motion and tensioning.

Method: Formulates self-wrap towing as a routing-aware, tensioning-implicit trajectory optimization (TITO) problem that couples: (1) a tensioning-implicit taut/slack constraint, and (2) routing-conditioned transmission maps for effective length and wrench. Builds a relaxation hierarchy with four approaches: strict mode-conditioned reference, Full-Mode Relaxation (FMR), Binary-Mode Relaxation (BMR), and Implicit-Mode Relaxation (IMR).

Result: Across planar towing tasks, making routing an explicit decision often yields conservative solutions that stay near switching boundaries, whereas Implicit-Mode Relaxation (IMR) induces self-wrap through state evolution and exploits the redirected torque channel whenever turning requires it.

Conclusion: The proposed TITO framework with implicit-mode relaxation effectively handles the complex dynamics of cable-towed manipulation with self-wrap contact, allowing the system to naturally exploit contact-induced wrench redirection rather than treating it as a constraint to avoid.

Abstract: Cable/rope elements are pervasive in deformable-object manipulation, often serving as a deformable force-transmission medium whose routing and contact determine how wrenches are delivered. In cable-towed manipulation, transmission is unilateral and hybrid: the tether can pull only when taut and becomes force-free when slack; in practice, the tether may also contact the object boundary and self-wrap around edges, which is not merely collision avoidance but a change of the wrench transmission channel by shifting the effective application point and moment arm, thereby coupling routing geometry with rigid-body motion and tensioning. We formulate self-wrap towing as a routing-aware, tensioning-implicit trajectory optimization (TITO) problem that couples (i) a tensioning-implicit taut/slack constraint and (ii) routing-conditioned transmission maps for effective length and wrench, and we build a relaxation hierarchy from a strict mode-conditioned reference to three tractable relaxations: Full-Mode Relaxation (FMR), Binary-Mode Relaxation (BMR), and Implicit-Mode Relaxation (IMR). Across planar towing tasks, we find that making routing an explicit decision often yields conservative solutions that stay near switching boundaries, whereas IMR induces self-wrap through state evolution and exploits the redirected torque channel whenever turning requires it.

</details>


### [252] [ReTac-ACT: A State-Gated Vision-Tactile Fusion Transformer for Precision Assembly](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.09565)
*Minchi Ruan,LiangQing Zhou,Hongtong Li,Zongtao Wang,ZhaoMing Lu,Jianwei Zhang,Bin Fang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: ReTac-ACT: A vision-tactile imitation learning policy for precision assembly that uses bidirectional cross-attention, occlusion-aware gating, and tactile reconstruction to achieve 90% success on peg-in-hole tasks at sub-millimeter clearances.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Precision assembly requires sub-millimeter corrections in "last-millimeter" regions where visual feedback fails due to occlusion from the end-effector and workpiece. Existing vision-only methods struggle in these contact-rich scenarios.

Method: Three synergistic mechanisms: (1) bidirectional cross-attention for reciprocal visuo-tactile feature enhancement before fusion, (2) proprioception-conditioned gating network that dynamically elevates tactile reliance when visual occlusion occurs, and (3) tactile reconstruction objective enforcing learning of manipulation-relevant contact information.

Result: Achieves 90% peg-in-hole success on NIST Assembly Task Board M1 benchmark, substantially outperforming vision-only and generalist baselines. Maintains 80% success at industrial-grade 0.1mm clearance. Ablation studies validate each component is indispensable.

Conclusion: ReTac-ACT effectively addresses precision assembly challenges in occluded contact-rich scenarios through synergistic vision-tactile fusion. The method and dataset will be released to support reproducible research in robotic manipulation.

Abstract: Precision assembly requires sub-millimeter corrections in contact-rich "last-millimeter" regions where visual feedback fails due to occlusion from the end-effector and workpiece. We present ReTac-ACT (Reconstruction-enhanced Tactile ACT), a vision-tactile imitation learning policy that addresses this challenge through three synergistic mechanisms: (i) bidirectional cross-attention enabling reciprocal visuo-tactile feature enhancement before fusion, (ii) a proprioception-conditioned gating network that dynamically elevates tactile reliance when visual occlusion occurs, and (iii) a tactile reconstruction objective enforcing learning of manipulation-relevant contact information rather than generic visual textures. Evaluated on the standardized NIST Assembly Task Board M1 benchmark, ReTac-ACT achieves 90% peg-in-hole success, substantially outperforming vision-only and generalist baseline methods, and maintains 80% success at industrial-grade 0.1mm clearance. Ablation studies validate that each architectural component is indispensable. The ReTac-ACT codebase and a vision-tactile demonstration dataset covering various clearance levels with both visual and tactile features will be released to support reproducible research.

</details>


### [253] [SCDP: Learning Humanoid Locomotion from Partial Observations via Mixed-Observation Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.09574)
*Milo Carroll,Tianhu Peng,Lingfan Bao,Chengxu Zhou,Zhibin Li*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: SCDP enables humanoid locomotion using only onboard sensors via sensor-conditioned diffusion policies, eliminating need for explicit state estimation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing humanoid locomotion methods rely on privileged full-body states requiring complex and unreliable state estimation. Need for deployable policies using only onboard sensors.

Method: Sensor-Conditioned Diffusion Policies (SCDP) with mixed-observation training: diffusion model conditions on sensor histories while supervised to predict privileged future state-action trajectories. Uses restricted denoising, context distribution alignment, and context-aware attention masking.

Result: In simulation: 99-100% success on velocity control, 93% tracking success on AMASS test set, comparable to privileged baselines. Successfully deployed on real G1 humanoid at 50 Hz without external sensing.

Conclusion: SCDP enables robust real-world humanoid locomotion using only onboard sensors, eliminating need for explicit state estimation while maintaining performance comparable to privileged methods.

Abstract: Distilling humanoid locomotion control from offline datasets into deployable policies remains a challenge, as existing methods rely on privileged full-body states that require complex and often unreliable state estimation. We present Sensor-Conditioned Diffusion Policies (SCDP) that enables humanoid locomotion using only onboard sensors, eliminating the need for explicit state estimation. SCDP decouples sensing from supervision through mixed-observation training: diffusion model conditions on sensor histories while being supervised to predict privileged future state-action trajectories, enforcing the model to infer the motion dynamics under partial observability. We further develop restricted denoising, context distribution alignment, and context-aware attention masking to encourage implicit state estimation within the model and to prevent train-deploy mismatch. We validate SCDP on velocity-commanded locomotion and motion reference tracking tasks. In simulation, SCDP achieves near-perfect success on velocity control (99-100%) and 93% tracking success in AMASS test set, performing comparable to privileged baselines while using only onboard sensors. Finally, we deploy the trained policy on a real G1 humanoid at 50 Hz, demonstrating robust real robot locomotion without external sensing or state estimation.

</details>


### [254] [Towards Terrain-Aware Safe Locomotion for Quadrupedal Robots Using Proprioceptive Sensing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.09585)
*Peiyu Yang,Jiatao Ding,Wei Pan,Claudio Semini,Cosimo Della Santina*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Proposes a proprioceptive-only terrain estimation and safety-critical control framework for quadrupedal robots on uneven terrain, integrating terrain mapping with control barrier functions for guaranteed safety.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Achieving safe quadrupedal locomotion on uneven terrain with low-cost robots using only proprioceptive sensors (IMUs, joint encoders, contact force sensors) is challenging due to unreliable estimation and lack of safety guarantees.

Method: 1) Develops estimation framework generating 2.5-D terrain map and extracting support plane parameters integrated into contact/state estimation. 2) Integrates estimation into safety-critical control using control barrier functions (CBFs) with rigorous safety guarantees.

Result: Terrain estimation provides smooth representations; coupled framework reduces base position estimation MAE by 64.8%, decreases variance by 47.2%, improves contact estimation robustness. Terrain-informed CBFs ensure global safety (avoiding hazardous areas) and local safety (preventing body-terrain collisions) using only proprioception.

Conclusion: The proposed proprioceptive-only framework successfully enables safe quadrupedal locomotion on uneven terrain through integrated terrain-state-contact estimation and safety-critical control with formal guarantees, demonstrating significant improvements over decoupled approaches.

Abstract: Achieving safe quadrupedal locomotion in real-world environments has attracted much attention in recent years. When walking over uneven terrain, achieving reliable estimation and realising safety-critical control based on the obtained information is still an open question. To address this challenge, especially for low-cost robots equipped solely with proprioceptive sensors (e.g., IMUs, joint encoders, and contact force sensors), this work first presents an estimation framework that generates a 2.5-D terrain map and extracts support plane parameters, which are then integrated into contact and state estimation. Then, we integrate this estimation framework into a safety-critical control pipeline by formulating control barrier functions that provide rigorous safety guarantees. Experiments demonstrate that the proposed terrain estimation method provides smooth terrain representations. Moreover, the coupled estimation framework of terrain, state, and contact reduces the mean absolute error of base position estimation by 64.8%, decreases the estimation variance by 47.2%, and improves the robustness of contact estimation compared to a decoupled framework. The terrain-informed CBFs integrate historical terrain information and current proprioceptive measurements to ensure global safety by keeping the robot out of hazardous areas and local safety by preventing body-terrain collision, relying solely on proprioceptive sensing.

</details>


### [255] [A Generalized Voronoi Graph based Coverage Control Approach for Non-Convex Environment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.09596)
*Zuyi Guo,Ronghao Zheng,Meiqin Liu,Senlin Zhang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Proposes a two-phase coverage control method using Generalized Voronoi Graph for multi-robot systems in non-convex regions with obstacles.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses the challenge of efficient coverage by multi-robot systems in complex, non-convex environments with multiple obstacles, where traditional methods may struggle with load balancing and coordination.

Method: Two-phase approach: 1) Load-Balancing Algorithm phase partitions non-convex region using Generalized Voronoi Graph and applies weighted load-balancing algorithm considering sub-region quality differences; 2) Collaborative Coverage phase uses new controllers for robots to effectively cover assigned sub-regions.

Result: Method convergence is mathematically proved, and performance is evaluated through simulations demonstrating effective load balancing and coverage in non-convex environments.

Conclusion: The proposed GVG-based coverage control method successfully addresses multi-robot coverage in complex environments through intelligent partitioning and load balancing, with proven convergence and validated performance.

Abstract: To address the challenge of efficient coverage by multi-robot systems in non-convex regions with multiple obstacles, this paper proposes a coverage control method based on the Generalized Voronoi Graph (GVG), which has two phases: Load-Balancing Algorithm phase and Collaborative Coverage phase. In Load-Balancing Algorithm phase, the non-convex region is partitioned into multiple sub-regions based on GVG. Besides, a weighted load-balancing algorithm is developed, which considers the quality differences among sub-regions. By iteratively optimizing the robot allocation ratio, the number of robots in each sub-region is matched with the sub-region quality to achieve load balance. In Collaborative Coverage phase, each robot is controlled by a new controller to effectively coverage the region. The convergence of the method is proved and its performance is evaluated through simulations.

</details>


### [256] [DRIFT: Dual-Representation Inter-Fusion Transformer for Automated Driving Perception with 4D Radar Point Clouds](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.09695)
*Siqi Pei,Andras Palffy,Dariu M. Gavrila*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: DRIFT is a dual-path 4D radar perception model that fuses local point features with global pillar features for improved object detection and free road estimation in autonomous driving.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: 4D radars are cost-effective and weather-robust but produce sparse point clouds compared to LiDAR, requiring better exploitation of both local and global contextual information for accurate perception.

Method: Dual-path architecture with parallel point path (fine-grained local features) and pillar path (coarse-grained global features), connected via novel feature-sharing layers at multiple stages for full representation utilization.

Result: Outperforms baselines on View-of-Delft and proprietary datasets for object detection and free road estimation. Achieves 52.6% mAP on VoD dataset vs 45.4% for CenterPoint.

Conclusion: DRIFT effectively captures and fuses both local and global contexts through its dual-path architecture, demonstrating superior performance for 4D radar perception tasks in autonomous driving systems.

Abstract: 4D radars, which provide 3D point cloud data along with Doppler velocity, are attractive components of modern automated driving systems due to their low cost and robustness under adverse weather conditions. However, they provide a significantly lower point cloud density than LiDAR sensors. This makes it important to exploit not only local but also global contextual scene information. This paper proposes DRIFT, a model that effectively captures and fuses both local and global contexts through a dual-path architecture. The model incorporates a point path to aggregate fine-grained local features and a pillar path to encode coarse-grained global features. These two parallel paths are intertwined via novel feature-sharing layers at multiple stages, enabling full utilization of both representations. DRIFT is evaluated on the widely used View-of-Delft (VoD) dataset and a proprietary internal dataset. It outperforms the baselines on the tasks of object detection and/or free road estimation. For example, DRIFT achieves a mean average precision (mAP) of 52.6\% (compared to, say, 45.4\% of CenterPoint) on the VoD dataset.

</details>


### [257] [Robotic Scene Cloning:Advancing Zero-Shot Robotic Scene Adaptation in Manipulation via Visual Prompt Editing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.09712)
*Binyuan Huang,Yuqing Wen,Yucheng Zhao,Yaosi Hu,Tiancai Wang,Chang Wen Chen,Haoqiang Fan,Zhenzhong Chen*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: RSC enables scene-specific robot adaptation by editing existing trajectories using visual prompting and condition injection, improving zero-shot generalization without extensive on-site data collection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Pre-trained robot models struggle with real-world deployment due to limited zero-shot capabilities, requiring extensive on-site data collection for adaptation to new environments.

Method: Robotic Scene Cloning (RSC) edits existing robot operation trajectories using visual prompting and a tuned condition injection module to transfer textures and perform shape adaptations based on visual prompts.

Result: RSC demonstrates reliable task performance across various object types and significantly enhances policy generalization in both simulated and real-world environments.

Conclusion: RSC provides an effective solution for scene-specific robot adaptation, reducing the need for extensive on-site data collection while improving generalization to target environments.

Abstract: Modern robots can perform a wide range of simple tasks and adapt to diverse scenarios in the well-trained environment. However, deploying pre-trained robot models in real-world user scenarios remains challenging due to their limited zero-shot capabilities, often necessitating extensive on-site data collection. To address this issue, we propose Robotic Scene Cloning (RSC), a novel method designed for scene-specific adaptation by editing existing robot operation trajectories. RSC achieves accurate and scene-consistent sample generation by leveraging a visual prompting mechanism and a carefully tuned condition injection module. Not only transferring textures but also performing moderate shape adaptations in response to the visual prompts, RSC demonstrates reliable task performance across a variety of object types. Experiments across various simulated and real-world environments demonstrate that RSC significantly enhances policy generalization in target environments.

</details>


### [258] [Let's Reward Step-by-Step: Step-Aware Contrastive Alignment for Vision-Language Navigation in Continuous Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.09740)
*Haoyuan Li,Rui Liu,Hehe Fan,Yi Yang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: SACA framework improves VLN-CE by using step-aware contrastive alignment to extract dense supervision from imperfect trajectories, addressing compounding errors in SFT and sparse rewards in RFT.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current VLN-CE training paradigms struggle with balancing generalization, error recovery, and training stability. SFT suffers from compounding errors and poor recovery from out-of-distribution states, while RFT methods are bottlenecked by sparse binary rewards that fail to assign credit to individual steps, leading to gradient collapse.

Method: SACA introduces step-aware contrastive alignment with two key components: 1) Perception-Grounded Step-Aware auditor that evaluates progress step-by-step and disentangles failed trajectories into valid prefixes and exact divergence points, and 2) Scenario-Conditioned Group Construction mechanism that dynamically routes batches to specialized resampling and optimization strategies.

Result: Extensive experiments on VLN-CE benchmarks demonstrate that SACA achieves state-of-the-art performance.

Conclusion: SACA effectively addresses the limitations of current VLN-CE training methods by extracting dense supervision from imperfect trajectories, enabling better generalization, error recovery, and training stability through step-aware contrastive alignment.

Abstract: Vision-Language Navigation in Continuous Environments (VLN-CE) requires agents to learn complex reasoning from long-horizon human interactions. While Multi-modal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have driven recent progress, current training paradigms struggle to balance generalization capability, error recovery and training stability. Specifically, (i) policies derived from SFT suffer from compounding errors, struggling to recover from out-of-distribution states, and (ii) Reinforcement Fine-Tuning (RFT) methods e.g. GRPO are bottlenecked by sparse outcome rewards. Their binary feedback fails to assign credit to individual steps, leading to gradient signal collapse in failure dominant batches. To address these challenges, we introduce Step-Aware Contrastive Alignment (SACA), a framework designed to extract dense supervision from imperfect trajectories. At its core, the Perception-Grounded Step-Aware auditor evaluates progress step-by-step, disentangling failed trajectories into valid prefixes and exact divergence points. Leveraging these signals, Scenario-Conditioned Group Construction mechanism dynamically routes batches to specialized resampling and optimization strategies. Extensive experiments on VLN-CE benchmarks demonstrate that SACA achieves state-of-the-art performance.

</details>


### [259] [Caterpillar-Inspired Spring-Based Compressive Continuum Robot for Bristle-based Exploration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.09745)
*Zhixian Hu,Yu She,Juan Wachs*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A compact spring-based tendon-driven continuum robot integrated with commercial arms for confined-space inspection, featuring coupled bending/axial motion and contact sensing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional rigid robots struggle with confined space exploration (pipelines, ducts) due to limited space, irregular geometry, and restricted access. Need for better inspection solutions.

Method: Spring-based tendon-driven continuum robot integrated with commercial robotic arms. Uses constant-curvature kinematic model for positional control. Integrates artificial bristle contact sensor for surface perception.

Result: Mean position error of 4.32 mm under proposed model and control pipeline. Demonstrated surface perception and confined-space exploration through contact interactions.

Conclusion: Compact, compliant design offers cost-effective upgrade for commercial robots and promises effective exploration in challenging confined environments.

Abstract: Exploration of confined spaces, such as pipelines and ducts, remains challenging for conventional rigid robots due to limited space, irregular geometry, and restricted access. Inspired by caterpillar locomotion and sensing, this paper presents a compact spring-based tendon-driven continuum robot that integrates with commercial robotic arms for confined-space inspection. The system combines a mechanically compliant continuum body with a tendon actuation module, enabling coupled bending and axial length change, and uses a constant-curvature kinematic model for positional control. Experiments show a mean position error of 4.32 mm under the proposed model and control pipeline. To extend the system from motion to inspection, we integrate an artificial bristle contact sensor and demonstrate surface perception and confined-space exploration through contact interactions. This compact and compliant design offers a cost-effective upgrade for commercial robots and promises effective exploration in challenging environments.

</details>


### [260] [MuxGel: Simultaneous Dual-Modal Visuo-Tactile Sensing via Spatially Multiplexing and Deep Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.09761)
*Zhixian Hu,Zhengtong Xu,Sheeraz Athar,Juan Wachs,Yu She*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: MuxGel is a vision-based tactile sensor that uses a checkerboard pattern to simultaneously capture external visual information and contact-induced tactile signals through a single camera, overcoming the traditional trade-off between tactile sensing and pre-contact vision.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Most vision-based tactile sensors face a fundamental trade-off: opaque coatings enable tactile sensing but block pre-contact vision, limiting robots' ability to both see objects before contact and sense them during manipulation.

Method: MuxGel uses a spatially multiplexed design with a checkerboard coating pattern that interleaves tactile-sensitive regions with transparent windows for external vision. A U-Net-based reconstruction framework, trained via sim-to-real pipeline, decouples and restores high-fidelity tactile and visual signals from the multiplexed camera inputs.

Result: Experiments on unseen objects demonstrate the framework's generalization and accuracy. MuxGel enhances grasping tasks by providing dual-modality feedback for both pre-contact alignment and post-contact interaction, while maintaining compatibility with existing GelSight-style hardware.

Conclusion: MuxGel successfully addresses the vision-tactile trade-off in robotic sensors, enabling simultaneous high-fidelity visual and tactile sensing through a single camera, which enhances robotic manipulation capabilities while maintaining hardware compatibility.

Abstract: High-fidelity visuo-tactile sensing is important for precise robotic manipulation. However, most vision-based tactile sensors face a fundamental trade-off: opaque coatings enable tactile sensing but block pre-contact vision. To address this, we propose MuxGel, a spatially multiplexed sensor that captures both external visual information and contact-induced tactile signals through a single camera. By using a checkerboard coating pattern, MuxGel interleaves tactile-sensitive regions with transparent windows for external vision. This design maintains standard form factors, allowing for plug-and-play integration into GelSight-style sensors by simply replacing the gel pad. To recover full-resolution vision and tactile signals from the multiplexed inputs, we develop a U-Net-based reconstruction framework. Leveraging a sim-to-real pipeline, our model effectively decouples and restores high-fidelity tactile and visual fields simultaneously. Experiments on unseen objects demonstrate the framework's generalization and accuracy. Furthermore, we demonstrate MuxGel's utility in grasping tasks, where dual-modality feedback facilitates both pre-contact alignment and post-contact interaction. Results show that MuxGel enhances the perceptual capabilities of existing vision-based tactile sensors while maintaining compatibility with their hardware stacks. Project webpage: https://zhixianhu.github.io/muxgel/.

</details>


### [261] [TIMID: Time-Dependent Mistake Detection in Videos of Robot Executions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.09782)
*Nerea Gallego,Fernando Salanova,Claudio Mannarano,Cristian Mahulea,Eduardo Montijano*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: TIMID: A video anomaly detection architecture for detecting time-dependent mistakes in robot task execution using weak supervision and temporal reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current Video Anomaly Detection (VAD) frameworks focus on low-level kinematic failures but struggle with complex temporal or spatial task violations that don't manifest as execution errors. There's a need to detect time-dependent mistakes in high-level robot tasks.

Method: TIMID architecture takes video and task/mistake prompts as input, outputs frame-level mistake detection. Uses VAD formulation for weak supervision (single label per video). Introduces multi-robot simulation dataset with controlled temporal errors for sim-to-real evaluation.

Result: Out-of-the-box Vision-Language Models (VLMs) lack explicit temporal reasoning for this task, but TIMID successfully detects different types of temporal errors in robot task execution.

Conclusion: TIMID addresses the gap in detecting complex temporal task violations in robotics, enabling detection of time-dependent mistakes through a weakly supervised approach with effective sim-to-real transfer.

Abstract: As robotic systems execute increasingly difficult task sequences, so does the number of ways in which they can fail. Video Anomaly Detection (VAD) frameworks typically focus on singular, low-level kinematic or action failures, struggling to identify more complex temporal or spatial task violations, because they do not necessarily manifest as low-level execution errors. To address this problem, the main contribution of this paper is a new VAD-inspired architecture, TIMID, which is able to detect robot time-dependent mistakes when executing high-level tasks. Our architecture receives as inputs a video and prompts of the task and the potential mistake, and returns a frame-level prediction in the video of whether the mistake is present or not. By adopting a VAD formulation, the model can be trained with weak supervision, requiring only a single label per video. Additionally, to alleviate the problem of data scarcity of incorrect executions, we introduce a multi-robot simulation dataset with controlled temporal errors and real executions for zero-shot sim-to-real evaluation. Our experiments demonstrate that out-of-the-box VLMs lack the explicit temporal reasoning required for this task, whereas our framework successfully detects different types of temporal errors. Project: https://ropertunizar.github.io/TIMID/

</details>


### [262] [Lightweight 3D LiDAR-Based UAV Tracking: An Adaptive Extended Kalman Filtering Approach](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.09783)
*Nivand Khosravi,Meysam Basiri,Rodrigo Ventura*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Lightweight LiDAR-based UAV tracking system using Adaptive Extended Kalman Filter (AEKF) for reliable relative positioning in GPS-denied environments, outperforming standard filtering methods under sparse point clouds and aggressive maneuvers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing vision-based tracking systems are sensitive to lighting conditions, while LiDAR-based methods often use bulky, power-intensive sensors unsuitable for small UAVs with strict payload and energy constraints. There's a need for robust tracking in GPS-denied environments without multi-sensor arrays or external infrastructure.

Method: Proposes a lightweight LiDAR-based UAV tracking system with an Adaptive Extended Kalman Filter (AEKF) framework that dynamically adjusts noise covariance matrices using innovation and residual statistics. Includes a recovery mechanism for tracking continuity during temporary detection failures from sparse LiDAR returns or occlusions. Uses Livox Mid-360 LiDAR on DJI F550 UAV.

Result: The method demonstrated robust UAV tracking performance under sparse LiDAR returns and intermittent detections, consistently outperforming both standard Kalman filtering and particle filtering approaches during aggressive maneuvers. Enables reliable relative positioning without multi-sensor arrays or external infrastructure.

Conclusion: The proposed AEKF-based LiDAR tracking system provides a practical solution for small UAVs with strict payload constraints, offering reliable relative positioning in GPS-denied environments by effectively handling sparse, noisy point cloud data from non-repetitive scanning LiDARs.

Abstract: Accurate relative positioning is crucial for swarm aerial robotics, enabling coordinated flight and collision avoidance. Although vision-based tracking has been extensively studied, 3D LiDAR-based methods remain underutilized despite their robustness under varying lighting conditions. Existing systems often rely on bulky, power-intensive sensors, making them impractical for small UAVs with strict payload and energy constraints. This paper presents a lightweight LiDAR-based UAV tracking system incorporating an Adaptive Extended Kalman Filter (AEKF) framework. Our approach effectively addresses the challenges posed by sparse, noisy, and nonuniform point cloud data generated by non-repetitive scanning 3D LiDARs, ensuring reliable tracking while remaining suitable for small drones with strict payload constraints. Unlike conventional filtering techniques, the proposed method dynamically adjusts the noise covariance matrices using innovation and residual statistics, thereby enhancing tracking accuracy under real-world conditions. Additionally, a recovery mechanism ensures continuity of tracking during temporary detection failures caused by scattered LiDAR returns or occlusions. Experimental validation was performed using a Livox Mid-360 LiDAR mounted on a DJI F550 UAV in real-world flight scenarios. The proposed method demonstrated robust UAV tracking performance under sparse LiDAR returns and intermittent detections, consistently outperforming both standard Kalman filtering and particle filtering approaches during aggressive maneuvers. These results confirm that the framework enables reliable relative positioning in GPS-denied environments without the need for multi-sensor arrays or external infrastructure.

</details>


### [263] [Emerging Extrinsic Dexterity in Cluttered Scenes via Dynamics-aware Policy Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.09882)
*Yixin Zheng,Jiangran Lyu,Yifan Zhang,Jiayi Chen,Mi Yan,Yuntian Deng,Xuesong Shi,Xiaoguang Zhao,Yizhou Wang,Zhizheng Zhang,He Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Dynamics-Aware Policy Learning (DAPL) framework enables extrinsic dexterity in cluttered environments by learning contact-induced object dynamics representations to guide reinforcement learning, outperforming existing methods by 25+% in simulation and achieving 50% success in real-world cluttered scenes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Extrinsic dexterity (using environmental contact) is limited in cluttered scenes due to complex coupled dynamics among multiple interacting objects. Existing approaches lack explicit modeling of these dynamics, limiting practical applicability in real-world cluttered environments.

Method: Dynamics-Aware Policy Learning (DAPL) framework that learns representations of contact-induced object dynamics through explicit world modeling, then uses these representations to condition reinforcement learning policies, eliminating the need for hand-crafted contact heuristics or complex reward shaping.

Result: Outperforms prehensile manipulation, human teleoperation, and prior representation-based policies by over 25% in success rate on unseen simulated cluttered scenes with varying densities. Achieves ~50% success rate across 10 real-world cluttered scenes, with practical grocery deployment demonstrating robust sim-to-real transfer.

Conclusion: DAPL enables effective extrinsic dexterity in cluttered environments by explicitly modeling complex contact dynamics, providing a practical solution for real-world manipulation tasks without requiring hand-crafted heuristics, with strong sim-to-real transfer capabilities.

Abstract: Extrinsic dexterity leverages environmental contact to overcome the limitations of prehensile manipulation. However, achieving such dexterity in cluttered scenes remains challenging and underexplored, as it requires selectively exploiting contact among multiple interacting objects with inherently coupled dynamics. Existing approaches lack explicit modeling of such complex dynamics and therefore fall short in non-prehensile manipulation in cluttered environments, which in turn limits their practical applicability in real-world environments. In this paper, we introduce a Dynamics-Aware Policy Learning (DAPL) framework that can facilitate policy learning with a learned representation of contact-induced object dynamics in cluttered environments. This representation is learned through explicit world modeling and used to condition reinforcement learning, enabling extrinsic dexterity to emerge without hand-crafted contact heuristics or complex reward shaping. We evaluate our approach in both simulation and the real world. Our method outperforms prehensile manipulation, human teleoperation, and prior representation-based policies by over 25% in success rate on unseen simulated cluttered scenes with varying densities. The real-world success rate reaches around 50% across 10 cluttered scenes, while a practical grocery deployment further demonstrates robust sim-to-real transfer and applicability.

</details>


### [264] [Robust Cooperative Localization in Featureless Environments: A Comparative Study of DCL, StCL, CCL, CI, and Standard-CL](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.09886)
*Nivand Khosravi,Meysam Basiri,Rodrigo Ventura*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Comparative study of 5 cooperative localization methods shows trade-offs between accuracy and consistency, with CI emerging as most balanced approach.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Need for accurate position estimation in multi-robot systems operating in GPS-denied environments, requiring understanding of trade-offs between different cooperative localization approaches for practical application selection.

Method: Comparative study of five CL approaches (CCL, DCL, StCL, CI, Standard-CL) implemented in ROS and evaluated through Monte Carlo simulations under weak data association and robust detection conditions.

Result: StCL and Standard-CL achieve lowest position errors but have severe filter inconsistency; DCL shows remarkable stability under challenging conditions; CI achieves near-optimal consistency with competitive accuracy; CCL provides theoretically optimal estimation but sensitive to outliers.

Conclusion: Different CL methods have fundamental trade-offs: CI emerges as most balanced approach, providing practical guidance for algorithm selection based on application requirements (accuracy vs. consistency vs. robustness).

Abstract: Cooperative localization (CL) enables accurate position estimation in multi-robot systems operating in GPS-denied environments. This paper presents a comparative study of five CL approaches: Centralized Cooperative Localization (CCL), Decentralized Cooperative Localization (DCL), Sequential Cooperative Localization (StCL), Covariance Intersection (CI), and Standard Cooperative Localization (Standard-CL). All methods are implemented in ROS and evaluated through Monte Carlo simulations under two conditions: weak data association and robust detection. Our analysis reveals fundamental trade-offs among the methods. StCL and Standard-CL achieve the lowest position errors but exhibit severe filter inconsistency, making them unsuitable for safety-critical applications. DCL demonstrates remarkable stability under challenging conditions due to its measurement stride mechanism, which provides implicit regularization against outliers. CI emerges as the most balanced approach, achieving near-optimal consistency while maintaining competitive accuracy. CCL provides theoretically optimal estimation but shows sensitivity to measurement outliers. These findings offer practical guidance for selecting CL algorithms based on application requirements.

</details>


### [265] [NanoBench: A Multi-Task Benchmark Dataset for Nano-Quadrotor System Identification, Control, and State Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.09908)
*Syed Izzat Ullah,Jose Baca*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: NanoBench is an open-source benchmark dataset for nano-quadrotor research, providing synchronized actuator-level signals, controller internals, and ground truth data for the Crazyflie 2.1 platform.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing aerial robotics benchmarks focus on larger vehicles and provide only high-level state data, missing the actuator-level signals needed for nano-scale quadrotors where low-Reynolds number aerodynamics, motor nonlinearities, and computational constraints invalidate models developed for larger vehicles.

Method: Collected over 170 flight trajectories on Crazyflie 2.1 nano-quadrotor in Vicon motion capture arena, providing synchronized Vicon ground truth, raw IMU data, onboard EKF estimates, PID controller internals, motor PWM commands at 100Hz, and battery telemetry at 10Hz with sub-0.5ms consistency.

Result: Created the first public dataset that jointly provides actuator commands, controller internals, and estimator outputs with millimeter-accurate ground truth on a commercially available nano-scale aerial platform, with standardized evaluation protocols and baselines for three tasks.

Conclusion: NanoBench fills a critical gap in aerial robotics benchmarking by providing comprehensive low-level data for nano-quadrotor research, enabling studies in nonlinear system identification, closed-loop controller benchmarking, and onboard state estimation assessment.

Abstract: Existing aerial-robotics benchmarks target vehicles from hundreds of grams to several kilograms and typically expose only high-level state data. They omit the actuator-level signals required to study nano-scale quadrotors, where low-Reynolds number aerodynamics, coreless DC motor nonlinearities, and severe computational constraints invalidate models and controllers developed for larger vehicles. We introduce NanoBench, an open-source multi-task benchmark collected on the commercially available Crazyflie 2.1 nano-quadrotor (takeoff weight 27 g) in a Vicon motion capture arena. The dataset contains over 170 flight trajectories spanning hover, multi-frequency excitation, standard tracking, and aggressive maneuvers across multiple speed regimes. Each trajectory provides synchronized Vicon ground truth, raw IMU data, onboard extended Kalman filter estimates, PID controller internals, and motor PWM commands at 100 Hz, alongside battery telemetry at 10 Hz, aligned with sub-0.5 ms consistency. NanoBench defines standardized evaluation protocols, train/test splits, and open-source baselines for three tasks: nonlinear system identification, closed-loop controller benchmarking, and onboard state estimation assessment. To our knowledge, it is the first public dataset to jointly provide actuator commands, controller internals, and estimator outputs with millimeter-accurate ground truth on a commercially available nano-scale aerial platform.

</details>


### [266] [Kinodynamic Motion Retargeting for Humanoid Locomotion via Multi-Contact Whole-Body Trajectory Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.09956)
*Xiaoyu Zhang,Steven Haener,Varun Madabushi,Maegan Tucker*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: KDMR is a novel humanoid locomotion framework that uses multi-contact whole-body trajectory optimization for motion retargeting, addressing physical inconsistencies in kinematic methods by enforcing dynamics and contact constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional kinematics-based retargeting methods relying solely on MoCap data introduce physically inconsistent artifacts like foot sliding and ground penetration, which degrade downstream imitation learning policies. There's a need to bridge this gap between pure kinematics and physical feasibility.

Method: KDMR models retargeting as a multi-contact, whole-body trajectory optimization problem that explicitly enforces rigid-body dynamics and contact complementarity constraints. It integrates ground reaction force (GRF) measurements alongside MoCap data to automatically detect heel-toe contact events and replicate complex human-like contact patterns.

Result: KDMR significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baseline GMR in three dimensions: 1) dynamic feasibility and smoothness of retargeted motions, 2) accuracy of GRF tracking compared to raw source data, and 3) training efficiency and final performance of downstream control policies trained via BeyondMimic framework.

Conclusion: KDMR yields dynamically viable reference trajectories that accelerate policy convergence and enhance overall locomotion stability. The end-to-end pipeline will be open-sourced upon publication, providing a superior alternative to purely kinematic retargeting methods.

Abstract: We present the KinoDynamic Motion Retargeting (KDMR) framework, a novel approach for humanoid locomotion that models the retargeting process as a multi-contact, whole-body trajectory optimization problem. Conventional kinematics-based retargeting methods rely solely on spatial motion capture (MoCap) data, inevitably introducing physically inconsistent artifacts, such as foot sliding and ground penetration, that severely degrade the performance of downstream imitation learning policies. To bridge this gap, KDMR extends beyond pure kinematics by explicitly enforcing rigid-body dynamics and contact complementarity constraints. Further, by integrating ground reaction force (GRF) measurements alongside MoCap data, our method automatically detects heel-toe contact events to accurately replicate complex human-like contact patterns. We evaluate KDMR against the state-of-the-art baseline, GMR, across three key dimensions: 1) the dynamic feasibility and smoothness of the retargeted motions, 2) the accuracy of GRF tracking compared to raw source data, and 3) the training efficiency and final performance of downstream control policies trained via the BeyondMimic framework. Experimental results demonstrate that KDMR significantly outperforms purely kinematic methods, yielding dynamically viable reference trajectories that accelerate policy convergence and enhance overall locomotion stability. Our end-to-end pipeline will be open-sourced upon publication.

</details>


### [267] [BEACON: Language-Conditioned Navigation Affordance Prediction under Occlusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.09961)
*Xinyu Gao,Gang Chen,Javier Alonso-Mora*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: BEACON predicts BEV affordance heatmaps for language-conditioned navigation, addressing occlusion issues that plague image-space VLMs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing vision-language spatial grounding methods struggle with occluded target locations because they rely on 2D image-space predictions tied to visible pixels, limiting their ability to infer targets behind obstacles like furniture or moving humans.

Method: BEACON predicts ego-centric Bird's-Eye View affordance heatmaps over bounded local regions including occluded areas. It takes surround-view RGB-D observations from four directions and instructions, injects spatial cues into a VLM, and fuses VLM outputs with depth-derived BEV features.

Result: BEACON improves accuracy averaged across geodesic thresholds by 22.74 percentage points over state-of-the-art image-space baselines on validation subsets with occluded target locations, using an occlusion-aware dataset built in Habitat simulator.

Conclusion: The BEV space formulation effectively addresses occlusion challenges in language-conditioned navigation, demonstrating significant improvements over image-space methods through careful module design and spatial cue injection.

Abstract: Language-conditioned local navigation requires a robot to infer a nearby traversable target location from its current observation and an open-vocabulary, relational instruction. Existing vision-language spatial grounding methods usually rely on vision-language models (VLMs) to reason in image space, producing 2D predictions tied to visible pixels. As a result, they struggle to infer target locations in occluded regions, typically caused by furniture or moving humans. To address this issue, we propose BEACON, which predicts an ego-centric Bird's-Eye View (BEV) affordance heatmap over a bounded local region including occluded areas. Given an instruction and surround-view RGB-D observations from four directions around the robot, BEACON predicts the BEV heatmap by injecting spatial cues into a VLM and fusing the VLM's output with depth-derived BEV features. Using an occlusion-aware dataset built in the Habitat simulator, we conduct detailed experimental analysis to validate both our BEV space formulation and the design choices of each module. Our method improves the accuracy averaged across geodesic thresholds by 22.74 percentage points over the state-of-the-art image-space baseline on the validation subset with occluded target locations. Our project page is: https://xin-yu-gao.github.io/beacon.

</details>


### [268] [TiPToP: A Modular Open-Vocabulary Planning System for Robotic Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.09971)
*William Shen,Nishanth Kumar,Sahit Chintalapudi,Jie Wang,Christopher Watson,Edward Hu,Jing Cao,Dinesh Jayaraman,Leslie Pack Kaelbling,Tomás Lozano-Pérez*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: TiPToP is a modular system combining pretrained vision models with Task and Motion Planning to solve multi-step manipulation tasks from RGB images and natural language, requiring zero robot data and outperforming fine-tuned VLA models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To create a simple, extensible system for multi-step manipulation tasks that doesn't require extensive robot-specific training data, bridging the gap between learning-based approaches and classical planning methods.

Method: Combines pretrained vision foundation models with existing Task and Motion Planning (TAMP) framework, using modular architecture that processes RGB images and natural language instructions to generate manipulation plans.

Result: Evaluated on 28 tabletop manipulation tasks in simulation and real world, matches or outperforms π₀.₅-DROID (a VLA model fine-tuned on 350 hours of demonstrations) despite requiring zero robot data.

Conclusion: TiPToP demonstrates that modular integration of pretrained vision models with classical planning can achieve state-of-the-art performance without robot-specific training data, enabling component-level failure analysis and open-sourced to advance research.

Abstract: We present TiPToP, an extensible modular system that combines pretrained vision foundation models with an existing Task and Motion Planner (TAMP) to solve multi-step manipulation tasks directly from input RGB images and natural-language instructions. Our system aims to be simple and easy-to-use: it can be installed and run on a standard DROID setup in under one hour and adapted to new embodiments with minimal effort. We evaluate TiPToP -- which requires zero robot data -- over 28 tabletop manipulation tasks in simulation and the real world and find it matches or outperforms $π_{0.5}\text{-DROID}$, a vision-language-action (VLA) model fine-tuned on 350 hours of embodiment-specific demonstrations. TiPToP's modular architecture enables us to analyze the system's failure modes at the component level. We analyze results from an evaluation of 173 trials and identify directions for improvement. We release TiPToP open-source to further research on modular manipulation systems and tighter integration between learning and planning. Project website and code: https://tiptop-robot.github.io

</details>


<div id='cs.IT'></div>

# cs.IT [[Back]](#toc)

### [269] [Feedback Does Not Increase the Capacity of Approximately Memoryless Surjective POST Channels](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.08886)
*Xiaojing Zhang,Jun Chen,Guanghui Wang*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: Feedback capacity equals non-feedback capacity for approximately memoryless POST channels under certain conditions, extending Shannon's theorem beyond strictly memoryless cases.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate whether feedback provides capacity gain for finite-state channels (POST channels) where the previous output serves as current state, extending Shannon's classical result beyond strictly memoryless channels.

Method: Study POST channels where state-dependent transition matrices are sufficiently close (approximately memoryless). Under surjectivity condition on associated memoryless reference channel, analyze capacity with and without feedback.

Result: Feedback capacity coincides with non-feedback capacity for approximately memoryless POST channels. For almost all such channels where input alphabet size ≥ output alphabet size, feedback provides no capacity gain.

Conclusion: Shannon's theorem on feedback not increasing capacity for discrete memoryless channels extends to approximately memoryless POST channels, showing the phenomenon holds well beyond strictly memoryless case.

Abstract: We study a class of finite-state channels, known as POST channels, in which the previous channel output serves as the current state. A POST channel is deemed approximately memoryless when the state-dependent transition matrices are sufficiently close to one another. For this family of channels, under a surjectivity condition on the associated memoryless reference channel, we show that the feedback capacity coincides with the non-feedback capacity. Consequently, for almost all approximately memoryless POST channels whose input alphabet size is no smaller than the output alphabet size, feedback provides no capacity gain. This result extends Shannon's classical theorem on discrete memoryless channels and demonstrates that the phenomenon holds well beyond the strictly memoryless case.

</details>


### [270] [A Note on the Equivalence Between Zero-knowledge and Quantum CSS Codes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.08941)
*Noga Ron-Zewi,Mor Weiss*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: Zero-knowledge codes and quantum CSS codes are shown to be equivalent, enabling construction of explicit asymptotically-good zero-knowledge locally-testable codes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Zero-knowledge codes (introduced in 1997) hide message information from few codeword symbols and have cryptographic applications. Quantum CSS codes (introduced in 1996) enable quantum error correction and have quantum complexity theory applications. The paper aims to explore connections between these two seemingly different types of error-correcting codes.

Method: The authors establish an equivalence between (linear, perfect) zero-knowledge codes and quantum CSS codes through theoretical analysis and mathematical proofs.

Result: The main result shows that zero-knowledge codes and quantum CSS codes are equivalent. This equivalence is then leveraged to construct explicit asymptotically-good zero-knowledge locally-testable codes.

Conclusion: The equivalence between zero-knowledge codes and quantum CSS codes provides a powerful connection between classical cryptography and quantum information theory, enabling new constructions of zero-knowledge codes with desirable properties like local testability.

Abstract: Zero-knowledge codes, introduced by Decatur, Goldreich, and Ron (ePrint 1997), are error-correcting codes in which few codeword symbols reveal no information about the encoded message, and have been extensively used in cryptographic constructions. Quantum CSS codes, introduced by Calderbank and Shor (Phys. Rev. A 1996) and Steane (Royal Society A 1996), are error-correcting codes that allow for quantum error correction, and are also useful for applications in quantum complexity theory. In this short note, we show that (linear, perfect) zero-knowledge codes and quantum CSS codes are equivalent. We demonstrate the potential of this equivalence by using it to obtain explicit asymptotically-good zero-knowledge locally-testable codes.

</details>


### [271] [Differentially Private Secure Multiplication: Beyond Two Multiplicands](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.08944)
*Haoyang Hu,Viveck R. Cadambe*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: Extends DP secure multiplication from 2 to M multiplicands with improved accuracy via polynomial encoding and noise cancellation, characterizing optimal trade-offs for different node regimes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior work only addressed DP secure multiplication for 2 multiplicands, but real-world distributed systems often need to compute products of many private inputs while maintaining differential privacy against colluding nodes.

Method: Proposes secure multiplication framework using carefully designed encoding polynomials with layered noise injection, enabling systematic cancellation of lower-order noise terms to improve accuracy.

Result: For (M-1)T+1 ≤ N ≤ MT, characterizes optimal privacy-accuracy trade-off; for N = T+1, derives asymptotically tight achievability and converse bounds in high-privacy regime.

Conclusion: Generalizes existing DP secure multiplication schemes to arbitrary M multiplicands with improved accuracy through noise cancellation, establishing fundamental trade-offs for different system scales.

Abstract: We study the problem of differentially private (DP) secure multiplication in distributed computing systems, focusing on regimes where perfect privacy and perfect accuracy cannot be simultaneously achieved. Specifically, N nodes collaboratively compute the product of M private inputs while guaranteeing epsilon-DP against any collusion of up to T nodes. Prior work has characterized the fundamental privacy-accuracy trade-off for the multiplication of two multiplicands. In this paper, we extend these results to the more general setting of computing the product of an arbitrary number M of multiplicands. We propose a secure multiplication framework based on carefully designed encoding polynomials combined with layered noise injection. The proposed construction generalizes existing schemes and enables the systematic cancellation of lower-order noise terms, leading to improved estimation accuracy. We explore two regimes: (M-1)T+1 <= N <= MT and N = T+1. For (M-1)T+1 <= N <= MT, we characterize the optimal privacy--accuracy trade-off. When N = T+1, we derive nontrivial achievability and converse bounds that are asymptotically tight in the high-privacy regime.

</details>


### [272] [Mitigation of UE Antenna Calibration Errors via Differential STBC in Cell-Free Massive MIMO](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.08962)
*Marx M. M. Freitas,Stefano Buzzi*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: DSTBC enables reliable downlink communication in cell-free massive MIMO without UE-side calibration or channel phase knowledge, mitigating antenna-dependent phase offsets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Antenna array calibration impairments at multi-antenna user equipment in cell-free massive MIMO downlink systems need to be addressed without requiring explicit UE-side calibration or channel phase knowledge.

Method: Proposes using differential space-time block coding (DSTBC) to enable reliable downlink communication without explicit UE-side calibration or channel phase knowledge.

Result: Simulation results show DSTBC-based transmission effectively mitigates antenna-dependent phase offsets and restores near-coherent performance in CF-mMIMO networks.

Conclusion: DSTBC provides a practical solution for addressing antenna calibration impairments in CF-mMIMO systems, enabling reliable communication without complex calibration procedures.

Abstract: This letter investigates the use of differential space-time block coding (DSTBC) to address antenna array calibration impairments at multi-antenna user equipment (UE) in the downlink (DL) of cell-free massive MIMO (CF-mMIMO) systems. We show that, by exploiting DSTBC, reliable DL communication can be achieved without explicit UE-side calibration or channel phase knowledge. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed DSTBC-based transmission effectively mitigates the impact of antenna-dependent phase offsets, restoring near-coherent performance in CF-mMIMO networks.

</details>
